title: Zopf
subtitle: Swiss Sunday braided loaf with a glossy butter-enriched crumb
excerpt: Zopf is the bread Swiss families make on Sunday: a three- or four-strand braid, buttery and soft, with a fine crumb and an eggwash gloss. It sits at the table throughout the day. The dough is enriched but not as heavy as brioche; the butter is folded in cold, which gives the crumb a layered, almost flaky quality when you pull it apart.
sourceNotes: Zopf (meaning braid or plait) is one of the most recognised Swiss breads. Recipes appear in Betty Bossi's 'Swiss Kitchen' and in standard Swiss housekeeping manuals from the 1950s onwards. The bread is closely related to the Austrian Striezel and the Jewish challah, all enriched braided loaves with roots in Central European baking tradition. The cold-butter technique comes from Alpine pastry practice where butter is incorporated into yeast dough without softening first.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze sliced for up to 1 month. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The shaped braid can prove overnight in the fridge, covered. Remove 30 minutes before baking.

glossaryTerms.definition: A yeast dough that contains fat, eggs, sugar, or dairy in addition to the basic flour-water-salt-yeast formula. The additions slow gluten development and inhibit the yeast, resulting in a longer rise and a tender, fine-grained crumb. Brioche, challah, and zopf are all enriched doughs.

content.text: The butter in zopf is incorporated cold, worked into the flour before the liquid is added. This is closer to a pastry technique than the usual method for
, and it gives the crumb a slightly layered, pull-apart quality. The dough is stiffer than brioche; it should feel firm and smooth, not sticky.

items.prepNote: large; 1 for the dough, 1 for the wash

content.text: Put 500 g flour, 9 g salt, 20 g sugar, and 7 g yeast in a large bowl. Add 100 g cold cubed butter and rub it in with your fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs with a few pea-sized pieces remaining; no need to work it as fine as pastry. Add 1 beaten egg and 150 ml warm milk. Mix to a dough, then turn onto the work surface.
Knead for 10 minutes until the dough is smooth, firm, and elastic. It will feel stiffer than a plain loaf dough. Cover and leave at room temperature for 75 minutes until doubled.

Divide into three equal pieces, each about 230 g. Roll each into a smooth strand, 50 cm long, tapered slightly at the ends. Braid from the centre out to each end for an even braid thickness. Transfer to a parchment-lined tray. Brush with the second beaten egg; use a light hand. Cover loosely and prove for 40 to 50 minutes until the braid is visibly puffed.

Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Give the braid a second light brush of egg. Bake for 28 to 32 minutes until deep golden. The loaf should sound hollow when tapped on the base and an internal probe should read 90 degrees C. Cool on a rack for 20 minutes before slicing.

items.problem: Braid unravelled and spread flat during baking
items.solution: The dough was over-proofed or the strands were not braided tightly enough. Braid firmly enough that the strands resist being pulled apart, and prove only until puffy, not doubled.

The cold butter pieces did not incorporate fully, creating fat pockets that interrupted the gluten network. Rub the butter in to a slightly finer texture, and ensure the dough is fully kneaded before the bulk ferment.
The egg wash was applied only once or too thinly. Two coats — one before proving, one just before the oven — give the glossy, deep-golden surface characteristic of zopf.
